abstract: We present Tongyi DeepResearch, an agentic large language model, which is specifically designed for long-horizon, deep information-seeking research tasks. To incentivize autonomous deep research agency, Tongyi DeepResearch is developed through an end-to-end training framework that combines agentic mid-training and agentic post-training, enabling scalable reasoning and information seeking across complex tasks. We design a highly scalable data synthesis pipeline that is fully automatic, without relying on costly human annotation, and empowers all training stages. By constructing customized environments for each stage, our system enables stable and consistent interactions throughout. Tongyi DeepResearch, featuring 30.5 billion total parameters, with only 3.3 billion activated per token, achieves state-of-the-art performance across a range of agentic deep research benchmarks, including Humanity's Last Exam, BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, WebWalkerQA, xbench-DeepSearch, FRAMES and xbench-DeepSearch-2510. We open-source the model, framework, and complete solutions to empower the community.

keyInsights: An "agentic inductive bias" can be systematically instilled into base models through staged continual pre-training and automated behavior data synthesis, effectively bridging foundational models to agentic capabilities.

Fully automated synthetic data generation can produce high-quality, complex agentic data, including QA pairs with injected uncertainty, effectively overcoming human annotation bottlenecks and enabling robust training for advanced agents.

Robust agentic reinforcement learning is achievable by combining algorithmic adaptations (tailored GRPO), an efficient asynchronous rollout framework, and a stable "unified sandbox" environment for reliable tool interaction in dynamic settings.

The Context Management paradigm, based on Markovian state reconstruction, effectively manages context length and enforces structured reasoning, which is crucial for scalable performance in long-horizon, multi-step research tasks.
originalProblem: Most advanced deep research agents are proprietary, rendering their intermediate processes and methodologies inaccessible to the broader AI community.
A lack of systematic methodology and publicly available open-source models for deep information-seeking and long-horizon research tasks hindered community-driven advancements.
Training agentic LLMs faces challenges such as the scarcity of high-quality, complex agentic data and the inherent instability of real-world environments during reinforcement learning.
results: The model achieved state-of-the-art performance on 6 agentic deep research benchmarks, outperforming both existing open-source and proprietary baselines, with scores such as 70.9 Avg@3 on GAIA and 43.4 Avg@3 on BrowseComp.
It demonstrated high computational efficiency, operating with 30.5 billion total parameters but activating only 3.3 billion parameters per token, making it more practical for deployment.
The introduced "Heavy Mode" test-time scaling further boosted performance, achieving Pass@1 scores of 38.3% on Humanity’s Last Exam and 58.3% on BrowseComp, by leveraging parallel agent exploration.
solution: An end-to-end training framework was developed, integrating "agentic mid-training" to instill an inductive bias and "agentic post-training" for refined deep research capabilities.
A Context Management paradigm was introduced within the ReAct framework, utilizing Markovian state reconstruction to prevent context overflow and enforce structured reasoning in long-horizon tasks.

The approach incorporates a fully automated pipeline for large-scale synthetic data generation, covering agent behavior, general function-calling, and complex, "super-human level" research questions for robust SFT and RL.
paper_summary.summary: Alibaba Group's Tongyi Lab developed Tongyi DeepResearch, an open-source agentic large language model designed for complex, long-horizon research. The model achieved state-of-the-art results on several challenging agentic benchmarks, including GAIA (70.9 Avg@3) and BrowseComp (43.4 Avg@3), while maintaining computational efficiency by activating only 3.3 billion parameters per token.

title: Tongyi DeepResearch Technical Report

abstract: As LLMs shift toward autonomous agents, Deep Research has emerged as a pivotal metric. However, existing academic benchmarks like BrowseComp often fail to meet real-world demands for open-ended research, which requires robust skills in intent recognition, long-horizon decision-making, and cross-source verification. To address this, we introduce Step-DeepResearch, a cost-effective, end-to-end agent. We propose a Data Synthesis Strategy Based on Atomic Capabilities to reinforce planning and report writing, combined with a progressive training path from agentic mid-training to SFT and RL. Enhanced by a Checklist-style Judger, this approach significantly improves robustness. Furthermore, to bridge the evaluation gap in the Chinese domain, we establish ADR-Bench for realistic deep research scenarios. Experimental results show that Step-DeepResearch (32B) scores 61.4% on Scale AI Research Rubrics. On ADR-Bench, it significantly outperforms comparable models and rivals SOTA closed-source models like OpenAI and Gemini DeepResearch. These findings prove that refined training enables medium-sized models to achieve expert-level capabilities at industry-leading cost-efficiency.

keyInsights: Expert-level Deep Research capabilities can be achieved by systematically training medium-sized models (32B parameters) on internalized atomic cognitive skills, rather than solely relying on larger model scales.

Reframing the training objective from next-token prediction to deciding the next 'Atomic Action' within a compact action subspace effectively enables long-horizon decision-making and complex reasoning.

A progressive training paradigm, combined with data synthesis focused on self-reflection and cross-validation, significantly enhances agent robustness and generalization across diverse, complex scenarios.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agents struggle with true "Deep Research" (open-ended, long-horizon tasks requiring logical structuring and cross-source verification), often acting as web crawlers rather than integrated researchers.

Current Deep Research systems are either workflow-based (high complexity, lack depth) or end-to-end (focus on search efficacy, not atomic cognitive capabilities, with high inference costs for larger models).
There is a scarcity of realistic, application-driven evaluation benchmarks for open-ended Deep Research, particularly for the Chinese domain, leading to an inaccurate assessment of practical utility.
results: Step-DeepResearch achieved a score of 61.42 on the ResearchRubrics benchmark, ranking as the No.1 ReAct Agent and second overall, at an inference cost of less than 0.50 RMB per invocation, which is over 10x cheaper than top commercial systems.

In human preference evaluations on the ADR-Bench (Chinese Deep Research benchmark), the model demonstrated significant non-inferiority, maintaining higher win rates against all competitors including Gemini and MiniMax.

The model showed strong performance across ResearchRubrics categories, leading in 'Implicit Criteria' (54.5) and 'Explicit Criteria' (72.0), tying for 'Citation Quality' (57.0), and outperforming counterparts in 'Communication Quality' (58.2).
solution: A 32B-parameter Qwen2.5-based model is trained using a novel "Atomic-capability Data Synthesis" strategy, explicitly targeting planning, deep search, reflection/verification, and report generation skills.

A multi-stage progressive training pipeline is employed, involving agentic mid-training (32K and 128K context), supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and reinforcement learning (RL) in a real tool-usage environment.

The system operates within a streamlined single-agent ReAct paradigm, supported by an architecture that includes authoritative information acquisition, knowledge management, and interactive execution with multimodal perception tools.
paper_summary.summary: Step-DeepResearch, developed by StepFun's Agent-Team, introduces a 32B-parameter model that utilizes a novel atomic-capability data synthesis and progressive training pipeline to perform complex "Deep Research" tasks. The model achieves expert-level performance on benchmarks while significantly reducing inference costs compared to larger, proprietary systems, demonstrating high cost-efficiency.

title: Step-DeepResearch Technical Report

abstract: We introduce Agentic Reasoning, a framework that enhances large language model (LLM) reasoning by integrating external tool-using agents. Agentic Reasoning dynamically leverages web search, code execution, and structured memory to address complex problems requiring deep research. A key innovation in our framework is the Mind-Map agent, which constructs a structured knowledge graph to store reasoning context and track logical relationships, ensuring coherence in long reasoning chains with extensive tool usage. Additionally, we conduct a comprehensive exploration of the Web-Search agent, leading to a highly effective search mechanism that surpasses all prior approaches. When deployed on DeepSeek-R1, our method achieves a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) among public models and delivers performance comparable to OpenAI Deep Research, the leading proprietary model in this domain. Extensive ablation studies validate the optimal selection of agentic tools and confirm the effectiveness of our Mind-Map and Web-Search agents in enhancing LLM reasoning. The code is at: this https URL

keyInsights: Integrating a small, systematically selected set of agentic tools (Web-Search, Code, Mind-Map) through careful design yields superior performance compared to simply adding more tools.
A structured external memory, like the Mind-Map's knowledge graph, is crucial for maintaining coherence, tracking complex logical relationships, and mitigating errors in long reasoning chains.
Delegating specific tasks to specialized LLM-based agents, rather than direct API calls, enhances robustness, overcomes token limitations, and improves accuracy on difficult problems.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with complex, multi-step, knowledge-intensive reasoning in unstructured or open-ended domains, often producing rigid or flawed results.
Existing multi-agent LLM frameworks often lack optimized task delegation, structured integration, and struggle with maintaining coherence over long reasoning chains.
A performance gap exists between powerful proprietary LLMs with advanced tool-use capabilities and accessible open-source models, whose methodologies are often undisclosed.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on the GPQA dataset (PhD-level science QA) with 81.2% accuracy, surpassing OpenAI o3-mini-high (79.7%) and improving base DeepSeek-R1 by nearly 10%.
Set a new SOTA among publicly available methods on the GAIA benchmark with 66.13% average accuracy, outperforming OpenAI Deep Research on Level 1 and 2 tasks.
Significantly outperformed RAG and search-based methods on the FreshWiki dataset for Wikipedia article generation, achieving ROUGE-1 of 54.10 compared to STORM's 47.93.
solution: A streamlined framework called "Agentic Reasoning" dynamically integrates three universally effective external LLM-based agents: Web-Search, Code Execution, and a Mind-Map memory agent.
The primary reasoning LLM (DeepSeek-R1) intelligently invokes these agents by embedding specialized tokens and queries into its reasoning sequence, with agent outputs seamlessly reintegrated.
A novel Mind-Map agent creates a structured knowledge graph from reasoning chains, providing organized external memory, context clustering, and queryable knowledge for improved coherence.
paper_summary.summary: The "Agentic Reasoning" framework enhances Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning by integrating streamlined external agents for web-search, code execution, and a novel Mind-Map memory. The approach enables open-source LLMs to achieve state-of-the-art performance, rivalling or surpassing proprietary models on complex expert-level problem-solving and deep research tasks.

title: Agentic Reasoning: A Streamlined Framework for Enhancing LLM Reasoning with Agentic Tools

abstract: We present Agent Lightning, a flexible and extensible framework that enables Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based training of Large Language Models (LLMs) for any AI agent. Unlike existing methods that tightly couple RL training with agent or rely on sequence concatenation with masking, Agent Lightning achieves complete decoupling between agent execution and training, allowing seamless integration with existing agents developed via diverse ways (e.g., using frameworks like LangChain, OpenAI Agents SDK, AutoGen, and building from scratch) with almost ZERO code modifications. By formulating agent execution as Markov decision process, we define an unified data interface and propose a hierarchical RL algorithm, LightningRL, which contains a credit assignment module, allowing us to decompose trajectories generated by ANY agents into training transition. This enables RL to handle complex interaction logic, such as multi-agent scenarios and dynamic workflows. For the system design, we introduce a Training-Agent Disaggregation architecture, and brings agent observability frameworks into agent runtime, providing a standardized agent finetuning interface. Experiments across text-to-SQL, retrieval-augmented generation, and math tool-use tasks demonstrate stable, continuous improvements, showcasing the framework's potential for real-world agent training and deployment.

keyInsights: Complete decoupling of agent execution from the reinforcement learning training process allows for the application of RL to diverse, pre-existing AI agents with minimal code changes.

Abstracting complex agent execution into a Markov Decision Process, where individual LLM invocations are treated as atomic actions, simplifies multi-turn RL without needing full trajectory concatenation.
Transparent collection of interaction data via observability tools (like OpenTelemetry) directly supports the RL training loop and facilitates automatic intermediate reward generation.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) used in AI agents often exhibit reliability issues and errors in complex, multi-step tasks or novel environments.

Existing Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods for LLMs are primarily designed for static, single-turn tasks and are not well-suited for the dynamic, multi-invocation, and tool-interacting nature of modern AI agents.
Integrating RL training with diverse agent implementations is challenging due to tight coupling between training frameworks and agent execution logic, demanding extensive code modifications.
results: Demonstrated stable and continuous performance improvement on a Text-to-SQL task implemented with LangChain, successfully optimizing specific components within a multi-agent system.
Achieved stable and continuous enhancement for a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) agent built with OpenAI Agents SDK, improving multi-hop question answering over a large document corpus.
Showed consistent performance improvement for a Math QA agent using AutoGen, validating its efficacy in precise tool-augmented reasoning workflows.
solution: A unified data interface abstracts any agent's execution into a Markov Decision Process (MDP), capturing LLM inputs, outputs, and rewards as individual transitions.

LightningRL, a hierarchical reinforcement learning algorithm, assigns episode-level credit to LLM actions and applies token-level supervision, avoiding the complexities of sequence concatenation and masking.

A Training-Agent Disaggregation (TA Disaggregation) architecture physically separates the RL training server from agent runtimes, enabling transparent data capture and automatic intermediate rewarding.
paper_summary.summary: Agent Lightning, developed by Microsoft Research, introduces a framework that completely decouples reinforcement learning training from agent execution, allowing for stable and continuous performance improvement of diverse AI agents with minimal code changes. This approach successfully optimized large language models in multi-turn tasks across LangChain, OpenAI Agents SDK, and AutoGen implementations.

title: Agent Lightning: Train ANY AI Agents with Reinforcement Learning

abstract:
Historically, scientific discovery has been a lengthy and costly process,
demanding substantial time and resources from initial conception to final
results. To accelerate scientific discovery, reduce research costs, and improve
research quality, we introduce Agent Laboratory, an autonomous LLM-based
framework capable of completing the entire research process. This framework
accepts a human-provided research idea and progresses through three
stages--literature review, experimentation, and report writing to produce
comprehensive research outputs, including a code repository and a research
report, while enabling users to provide feedback and guidance at each stage. We
deploy Agent Laboratory with various state-of-the-art LLMs and invite multiple
researchers to assess its quality by participating in a survey, providing human
feedback to guide the research process, and then evaluate the final paper. We
found that: (1) Agent Laboratory driven by o1-preview generates the best
research outcomes; (2) The generated machine learning code is able to achieve
state-of-the-art performance compared to existing methods; (3) Human
involvement, providing feedback at each stage, significantly improves the
overall quality of research; (4) Agent Laboratory significantly reduces
research expenses, achieving an 84% decrease compared to previous autonomous
research methods. We hope Agent Laboratory enables researchers to allocate more
effort toward creative ideation rather than low-level coding and writing,
ultimately accelerating scientific discovery.

keyInsights: Human-AI collaboration, particularly in a "co-pilot" model, is effective for improving the quality of AI-generated research outputs.
Automated evaluation of research quality often overestimates results compared to human assessment, underscoring the need for human validation.
The choice of LLM backend significantly impacts research outcomes, with `o1-preview` and `o1-mini` performing better for specific tasks than `gpt-4o`.
originalProblem: Scientific research is a lengthy, resource-intensive process that limits the number of ideas researchers can explore simultaneously.
Researchers are often burdened by repetitive, low-level tasks, hindering their focus on creative ideation and high-level experiment design.
The high cost and time investment in conducting research create a bottleneck in accelerating scientific discovery.
results: Reduced research costs by 84%, with a full paper generation costing as little as $2.33 using `gpt-4o`, which also completed the workflow in approximately 19.4 minutes.
The `mle-solver` component achieved state-of-the-art performance on MLE-Bench Kaggle challenges, earning more medals than competitors and surpassing median human performance on 60% of benchmarks.
Human intervention in "co-pilot mode" notably improved the overall quality of research outputs, with papers rated higher (4.38/10) compared to autonomously generated papers (3.8/10).
solution: An LLM-based multi-agent framework orchestrates the entire research process through distinct sequential phases: Literature Review, Experimentation, and Report Writing.
Specialized agents (e.g., PhD, ML Engineer) collaborate within the pipeline, utilizing external tools and iterative refinement loops powered by modules like `mle-solver` and `paper-solver`.
The framework operates in both autonomous mode and a human-guided "co-pilot" mode, allowing researchers to intervene at checkpoints and provide feedback to refine agent performance.
paper_summary.summary: Agent Laboratory, an LLM-based framework from AMD and academic partners, automates the scientific research process, notably reducing research costs to $2.33 per paper and enabling state-of-the-art performance in automated machine learning problem-solving. Human involvement in its co-pilot mode improves the overall quality of research outputs.

title: Agent Laboratory: Using LLM Agents as Research Assistants

abstract: One of the grand challenges of artificial general intelligence is developing agents capable of conducting scientific research and discovering new knowledge. While frontier models have already been used as aides to human scientists, e.g. for brainstorming ideas, writing code, or prediction tasks, they still conduct only a small part of the scientific process. This paper presents the first comprehensive framework for fully automatic scientific discovery, enabling frontier large language models to perform research independently and communicate their findings. We introduce The AI Scientist, which generates novel research ideas, writes code, executes experiments, visualizes results, describes its findings by writing a full scientific paper, and then runs a simulated review process for evaluation. In principle, this process can be repeated to iteratively develop ideas in an open-ended fashion, acting like the human scientific community. We demonstrate its versatility by applying it to three distinct subfields of machine learning: diffusion modeling, transformer-based language modeling, and learning dynamics. Each idea is implemented and developed into a full paper at a cost of less than $15 per paper. To evaluate the generated papers, we design and validate an automated reviewer, which we show achieves near-human performance in evaluating paper scores. The AI Scientist can produce papers that exceed the acceptance threshold at a top machine learning conference as judged by our automated reviewer. This approach signifies the beginning of a new era in scientific discovery in machine learning: bringing the transformative benefits of AI agents to the entire research process of AI itself, and taking us closer to a world where endless affordable creativity and innovation can be unleashed on the world's most challenging problems. Our code is open-sourced at this https URL

keyInsights: AI agents can autonomously perform the entire scientific research lifecycle, from ideation and experimentation to paper writing and simulated peer review.
Integrating LLMs with external tools (like Aider for coding and Semantic Scholar for literature search) enables robust, iterative experimentation and knowledge synthesis.

Automated LLM-based peer reviewers can achieve near-human-level accuracy in evaluating scientific papers, offering a scalable solution for quality control and feedback in an autonomous research ecosystem.
originalProblem: Traditional human-driven scientific discovery is slow, labor-intensive, and limited by human ingenuity and time, hindering the pace of knowledge generation.
Existing AI applications in science primarily assist human researchers or automate constrained tasks, lacking an end-to-end framework for fully autonomous research.
There is a need to democratize scientific research by lowering financial and intellectual barriers, accelerating progress across various domains.
results: The AI Scientist generated hundreds of medium-quality papers at an approximate cost of $10-$15 per paper, leveraging LLM API costs for coding and writing.
A GPT-4o-based automated reviewer achieved near-human-level balanced accuracy (0.65) and a superhuman F1 Score (0.57) compared to human reviewers on ICLR 2022 papers, costing $0.25-$0.50 per review.

The system successfully implemented complex algorithmic changes and generated novel visualizations in case studies (e.g., 'Adaptive Dual-Scale Denoising'), though it exhibited issues like subtle code errors, hallucinations, and a positive interpretation bias.
solution: An end-to-end framework called "The AI Scientist" was developed, leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) to perform iterative cycles of idea generation, experimental iteration, and scientific paper write-up.
The system integrates tools like Aider for autonomous code generation and execution, and connects to the Semantic Scholar API for novelty checks, related work, and citation management.
An LLM-based automated reviewer mimics conference guidelines to evaluate generated papers, providing numerical scores and textual feedback that can loop back into the discovery process.
paper_summary.summary: Sakana AI and collaborating institutions developed a comprehensive framework for fully automated scientific discovery, enabling AI agents to conduct an entire research endeavor from hypothesis to peer-reviewed paper. This system generated hundreds of medium-quality papers in machine learning at a cost of under $15 per paper, with its automated reviewer achieving near-human-level performance.

title: The AI Scientist: Towards Fully Automated Open-Ended Scientific Discovery

abstract: Recent advances in deep-research systems have demonstrated the potential for AI agents to autonomously discover and synthesize knowledge from external sources. In this paper, we introduce WebResearcher, a novel framework for building such agents through two key components: (1) WebResearcher, an iterative deep-research paradigm that reformulates deep research as a Markov Decision Process, where agents periodically consolidate findings into evolving reports while maintaining focused workspaces, overcoming the context suffocation and noise contamination that plague existing mono-contextual approaches; and (2) WebFrontier, a scalable data synthesis engine that generates high-quality training data through tool-augmented complexity escalation, enabling systematic creation of research tasks that bridge the gap between passive knowledge recall and active knowledge construction. Notably, we find that the training data from our paradigm significantly enhances tool-use capabilities even for traditional mono-contextual methods. Furthermore, our paradigm naturally scales through parallel thinking, enabling concurrent multi-agent exploration for more comprehensive conclusions. Extensive experiments across 6 challenging benchmarks demonstrate that WebResearcher achieves state-of-the-art performance, even surpassing frontier proprietary systems.

keyInsights: Redefining deep research as a Markov Decision Process with iterative synthesis and workspace reconstruction effectively mitigates context degradation and error propagation.
High-quality training data, generated by systematically escalating cognitive complexity with tool use, is essential and can significantly enhance agent capabilities.
Leveraging a multi-agent Research-Synthesis Framework at inference time improves robustness and accuracy by integrating diverse reasoning paths.
originalProblem: Existing deep-research AI agents suffer from "cognitive workspace suffocation" due to continuously expanding context windows in their mono-contextual paradigm.
Irreversible noise contamination and accumulation of irrelevant information degrade reasoning quality over long research horizons.
There is a critical scarcity of high-quality, complex training data for developing robust tool-augmented deep-research agents.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance across six challenging benchmarks, outperforming larger general LLMs and existing proprietary/open-source deep-research systems.
Secured 36.7% accuracy on Humanity's Last Exam, representing a 6.9 percentage point lead over the next best system, and 75.7% accuracy on GAIA, a 9.7 percentage point improvement over DeepSeek-V3.1.
Ablation studies confirmed the Iterative Deep-Research Paradigm as the critical performance driver, and the Research-Synthesis Framework improved HLE pass@1 from 28.8% to 35.65% with parallel agents.
solution: Introduction of IterResearch, an iterative deep-research paradigm that reformulates research as a Markov Decision Process, enabling periodic synthesis of findings and reconstruction of focused workspaces.
Development of WebFrontier, a scalable data synthesis engine, which generates large volumes of high-quality, complex, and verifiable training data through a multi-agent iterative workflow.
Implementation of a Research-Synthesis Framework at test-time, utilizing parallel research agents and a synthesis agent to integrate diverse findings for more comprehensive and robust conclusions.
paper_summary.summary: WebResearcher, an AI agent framework from Alibaba Group's Tongyi Lab, introduces an iterative deep-research paradigm and a scalable data synthesis engine to overcome context limitations in long-horizon tasks. It demonstrated state-of-the-art performance across six challenging benchmarks, achieving a 6.9 percentage point improvement on Humanity's Last Exam and a 9.7 percentage point lead on GAIA over previous top systems.

title: WebResearcher: Unleashing unbounded reasoning capability in Long-Horizon Agents

abstract: Equipping large language models (LLMs) with complex, interleaved reasoning and tool-use capabilities has become a key focus in agentic AI research, especially with recent advances in reasoning-oriented (``thinking'') models. Such capabilities are key to unlocking a number of important applications. One such application is Deep Research (DR), which requires extensive search and reasoning over many sources. Our work in this paper focuses on the development of native Autonomous Single-Agent models for DR featuring minimal web crawling and Python tool integration. Unlike multi-agent systems, where agents take up pre-defined roles and are told what to do at each step in a static workflow, an autonomous single-agent determines its next action dynamically based on context, without manual directive. While prior work has proposed training recipes for base or instruction-tuned LLMs, we focus on continual reinforcement learning (RL) of reasoning-optimized models to further enhance agentic skills while preserving reasoning ability. Towards this end, we propose a simple RL recipe with entirely synthetic data, which we apply to various open-source LLMs. Our best variant SFR-DR-20B achieves up to 28.7% on Humanity's Last Exam benchmark. In addition, we conduct key analysis experiments to provide more insights into our methodologies.

keyInsights: Reframing multi-turn conversations into iterative single-turn tasks with selective Chain-of-Thought (CoT) inclusion is crucial for preserving and enhancing the reasoning capabilities of certain LLM architectures.

Length normalization in the RL advantage calculation is essential for stable training, preventing degenerate behaviors like repetitive tool calls and ensuring performance improvements for long-horizon tasks.
Fault tolerance mechanisms (repair, retry, error messages) are critical for robust agent operation, allowing models to self-correct and complete trajectories successfully.
originalProblem: Equipping Large Language Models (LLMs) with advanced reasoning and tool-use to perform complex, long-horizon tasks, mitigating issues like hallucination.
Developing effective autonomous single-agent systems for Deep Research (DR) that can operate without rigid multi-agent orchestrations, offering better generalization.
Stabilizing Reinforcement Learning (RL) for multi-turn agentic tasks where traditional methods struggle with stability and can degrade base model reasoning abilities.
results: SFR-DR-20B achieved a 28.7% score on the full text-only HLE benchmark, marking a 65% relative improvement over its gpt-oss-20b base model, and outperformed proprietary agents like OpenAI Deep Research o3.
The framework set new state-of-the-art performance on FRAMES with 82.8% and GAIA with 66.0%, surpassing all reported baselines, including proprietary and multi-agent systems.
Length normalization in RL prevented unstable training and a decline in performance observed when agents degenerated into making repetitive, identical tool calls.
solution: Developed an Agentic Inference Scaffolding with a minimal set of three tools (`search_internet`, `browse_page`, `code_interpreter`) and an iterative single-turn workflow for specific LLM families.
Created a novel synthetic data generation pipeline to produce challenging multi-hop QA and long-form report writing tasks for agentic training.

Implemented an End-to-End Reinforcement Learning recipe featuring a length-normalized REINFORCE objective, trajectory filtering, and partial rollouts to ensure stable policy optimization for multi-turn interactions.
paper_summary.summary: Salesforce AI Research introduced SFR-DeepResearch, a framework utilizing continual reinforcement learning to enhance the autonomous reasoning and tool-use capabilities of single-agent Large Language Models. This approach yielded a 65% relative improvement over its base model on the HLE benchmark and achieved state-of-the-art performance on FRAMES and GAIA, surpassing several proprietary and multi-agent systems.

title: SFR-DeepResearch: Towards Effective Reinforcement Learning for Autonomously Reasoning Single Agents

abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based evolution is a promising approach for open-ended discovery, where progress requires sustained search and knowledge accumulation. Existing methods still rely heavily on fixed heuristics and hard-coded exploration rules, which limit the autonomy of LLM agents. We present CORAL, the first framework for autonomous multi-agent evolution on open-ended problems. CORAL replaces rigid control with long-running agents that explore, reflect, and collaborate through shared persistent memory, asynchronous multi-agent execution, and heartbeat-based interventions. It also provides practical safeguards, including isolated workspaces, evaluator separation, resource management, and agent session and health management. Evaluated on diverse mathematical, algorithmic, and systems optimization tasks, CORAL sets new state-of-the-art results on 10 tasks, achieving 3-10 times higher improvement rates with far fewer evaluations than fixed evolutionary search baselines across tasks. On Anthropic's kernel engineering task, four co-evolving agents improve the best known score from 1363 to 1103 cycles. Mechanistic analyses further show how these gains arise from knowledge reuse and multi-agent exploration and communication. Together, these results suggest that greater agent autonomy and multi-agent evolution can substantially improve open-ended discovery. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Granting LLM agents autonomy over evolutionary decisions and facilitating local verification steps leads to substantially more efficient search and higher improvement rates compared to fixed evolutionary algorithms.

Asynchronous multi-agent organization with shared persistent memory fosters emergent collaboration and diverse exploration, extending the search frontier beyond what single autonomous agents can achieve.

The systematic accumulation and sharing of knowledge, via agent-generated notes and reusable skills, is critical for driving successful and sustained discovery, especially in complex open-ended tasks.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-powered evolutionary search relies on fixed heuristics and external rules, limiting agent autonomy and adaptability in complex, open-ended problem spaces where optimal search strategies are unknown.

Current multi-agent LLM systems often use 'vertical scaling' with human-defined roles and rigid communication, which is restrictive for truly open-ended discovery where optimal task decomposition and interaction patterns are not known in advance.
results: A single CORAL agent outperformed fixed evolutionary search baselines on all 11 mathematical and systems optimization tasks, achieving new state-of-the-art results on 8 tasks and demonstrating a 3-10x higher improvement rate.

Multi-agent CORAL (4 agents) extended search performance on challenging stress-test problems, achieving an 18.3% cycle reduction in kernel engineering (a new SOTA) and a 5.0% score increase in polyominoes packing.

Analysis revealed that multi-agent gains stem from cross-agent information transfer (e.g., 36% of attempts on Kernel Engineering used cross-agent parents with higher improvement rates) and increased exploration diversity (low Jaccard similarity of strategies across agents).
solution: CORAL implements autonomous multi-agent evolution by empowering LLM agents with control over the retrieve, propose, evaluate, and update stages of the evolutionary loop, rather than relying on fixed external rules.

A shared persistent memory, structured as a file system, enables asynchronous multi-agent organization where agents explore solution spaces in parallel and indirectly exchange discoveries, notes, and reusable skills.

Heartbeat-based interventions (e.g., periodic consolidation, stagnation redirection) are incorporated to prevent agents from getting stuck, encourage knowledge contribution, and dynamically reorient search strategies.
paper_summary.summary: The CORAL framework introduces an autonomous multi-agent evolutionary system, empowering Large Language Model agents to control their discovery process and collaborate indirectly through shared persistent memory. This approach achieved new state-of-the-art results on 8 out of 11 mathematical and systems optimization tasks, exhibiting a 3-10x higher improvement rate and an 18.3% cycle reduction in kernel engineering.

title: CORAL: Towards Autonomous Multi-Agent Evolution for Open-Ended Discovery

abstract:
The powerful reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) in
mathematics and coding, combined with their ability to automate complex tasks
through agentic frameworks, present unprecedented opportunities for
accelerating scientific innovation. In this paper, we introduce AI-Researcher,
a fully autonomous research system that transforms how AI-driven scientific
discovery is conducted and evaluated. Our framework seamlessly orchestrates the
complete research pipeline--from literature review and hypothesis generation to
algorithm implementation and publication-ready manuscript preparation--with
minimal human intervention. To rigorously assess autonomous research
capabilities, we develop Scientist-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark comprising
state-of-the-art papers across diverse AI research domains, featuring both
guided innovation and open-ended exploration tasks. Through extensive
experiments, we demonstrate that AI-Researcher achieves remarkable
implementation success rates and produces research papers that approach
human-level quality. This work establishes new foundations for autonomous
scientific innovation that can complement human researchers by systematically
exploring solution spaces beyond cognitive limitations.

keyInsights: Fully autonomous AI systems can orchestrate the entire scientific discovery pipeline, from conceptualization to publication-ready manuscript generation, with high fidelity.

Open-ended autonomous exploration tasks (Level-2) can yield superior scientific quality compared to instruction-guided tasks (Level-1), suggesting that prescriptive instructions might constrain AI creativity.

The choice of the foundational LLM backbone significantly impacts both the implementation success rates and the scientific quality of the generated research, with Claude-series models often outperforming GPT-4o for this task.
originalProblem: Human researchers face limitations (cognitive biases, processing capacity) in navigating the vast and complex landscape of scientific discovery.
Existing AI agent systems, while automating routine tasks, lack the deep intellectual capacity and end-to-end orchestration needed for genuine scientific innovation.
A critical absence of standardized benchmarks hinders systematic evaluation and progress in autonomous scientific research systems.
results: AI-Researcher achieved a 93.8% code implementation completeness rate with Claude-series models on Scientist-Bench, successfully producing executable code for diverse tasks.
AI-generated papers approached human-level quality, with 15.79% to 78.95% (depending on the LLM evaluator) deemed comparable to human-authored top-tier research.

The system demonstrated superior scientific quality in open-ended (Level-2) innovation tasks, achieving higher average ratings and comparable rates (e.g., 40-100% comparable) than instruction-guided (Level-1) tasks.
solution: Introduces AI-Researcher, a multi-agent framework that autonomously orchestrates the complete scientific discovery lifecycle, from literature review and hypothesis generation to algorithm implementation, experimental validation, and manuscript preparation.
Develops a Multi-Stage Refinement Architecture and Expert Validation Framework that mimics human iterative research, enhancing implementation success rates and allowing for test-time scaling.

Presents Scientist-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark featuring Level-1 (guided innovation) and Level-2 (autonomous exploration) tasks, along with a two-stage evaluation protocol for technical execution and scientific contribution.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at The University of Hong Kong developed AI-Researcher, a fully autonomous AI system designed to conduct end-to-end scientific discovery, and introduced Scientist-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating autonomous research capabilities. The system achieved high code implementation rates and generated research papers that approached human-level quality, particularly excelling in open-ended scientific innovation tasks.

title: AI-Researcher: Autonomous Scientific Innovation

abstract: This paper introduces Agent-Based Auto Research, a structured multi-agent framework designed to automate, coordinate, and optimize the full lifecycle of scientific research. Leveraging the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) and modular agent collaboration, the system spans all major research phases, including literature review, ideation, methodology planning, experimentation, paper writing, peer review response, and dissemination. By addressing issues such as fragmented workflows, uneven methodological expertise, and cognitive overload, the framework offers a systematic and scalable approach to scientific inquiry. Preliminary explorations demonstrate the feasibility and potential of Auto Research as a promising paradigm for self-improving, AI-driven research processes.

keyInsights: The work redefines scientific inquiry as a modular, interpretable, and optimizable process, enabling a systemic integration of human and machine intelligence.
LLM agents can effectively coordinate and automate complex research tasks across the full lifecycle, from generating novel ideas to strategizing research dissemination.
This framework promises to democratize research access, accelerate scientific progress, and enhance reproducibility by mitigating human limitations and cognitive overload.
originalProblem: Traditional scientific research suffers from fragmented workflows, leading to inefficiencies and increased cognitive burden for researchers.
There is an uneven distribution of methodological expertise and limited systematization in problem-solving, impacting research quality and reproducibility.
Researchers experience cognitive overload due to the vast amount of information and diverse skill sets required, hindering deep engagement with core problems.
results: LLM agents successfully clarified research topics, identified gaps in literature, and generated initial LaTeX drafts for literature reviews.
A prototype method planner generated logical research plans, and a heuristic solution designer selected practical techniques for sub-tasks, such as using LSTM for cryptomining detection.

Experimental agents correctly identified appropriate benchmarks for 90% of CV/NLP tasks, aligned baselines with human selections in 78% of cases, and produced executable Python code (72-78% unmodified).
solution: A multi-agent framework is introduced, employing specialized LLM agents for each of the eight phases of the research lifecycle (Literature, Idea, Method, Experiment, Paper, Evaluation, Rebuttal, Promotion).
Agents leverage LLMs for natural language understanding, content generation, and Chain-of-Thought reasoning to decompose problems and make heuristic-driven decisions.
The system dynamically retrieves information from real-time search engines and scholarly databases to ensure up-to-date knowledge and supports iterative refinement and self-optimization.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Nanyang Technological University and Nankai University propose an "Agent-Based Auto Research" framework, which utilizes a multi-agent system powered by Large Language Models to automate the entire scientific inquiry lifecycle. Preliminary explorations across eight research phases demonstrate the feasibility of agents managing tasks from literature review and ideation to experimental setup, paper drafting, peer review simulation, and research dissemination.

title: A Vision for Auto Research with LLM Agents

abstract:
In this work, we investigate the potential of large language models (LLMs)
based agents to automate data science tasks, with the goal of comprehending
task requirements, then building and training the best-fit machine learning
models. Despite their widespread success, existing LLM agents are hindered by
generating unreasonable experiment plans within this scenario. To this end, we
present DS-Agent, a novel automatic framework that harnesses LLM agent and
case-based reasoning (CBR). In the development stage, DS-Agent follows the CBR
framework to structure an automatic iteration pipeline, which can flexibly
capitalize on the expert knowledge from Kaggle, and facilitate consistent
performance improvement through the feedback mechanism. Moreover, DS-Agent
implements a low-resource deployment stage with a simplified CBR paradigm to
adapt past successful solutions from the development stage for direct code
generation, significantly reducing the demand on foundational capabilities of
LLMs. Empirically, DS-Agent with GPT-4 achieves 100\% success rate in the
development stage, while attaining 36\% improvement on average one pass rate
across alternative LLMs in the deployment stage. In both stages, DS-Agent
achieves the best rank in performance, costing \$1.60 and \$0.13 per run with
GPT-4, respectively. Our data and code are open-sourced at
https://github.com/guosyjlu/DS-Agent.

keyInsights: Integrating Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) with LLMs effectively mitigates common LLM agent issues like hallucination and unreliable plan generation in complex data science tasks.
An iterative refinement loop, where the system revises experiment plans based on execution feedback, consistently improves performance over time.

Learning from successful agent-generated code solutions in a dedicated 'agent case bank' is more effective for efficient deployment than relying solely on textual human insights, especially when providing only one example.
originalProblem: Existing Large Language Model (LLM) agents struggle with generating reasonable experiment plans and suffer from hallucination issues in complex, open-ended data science tasks, leading to low completion rates.
Fine-tuning LLMs for data science is computationally expensive and challenging due to the difficulty in collecting high-quality training samples that require time-consuming code execution feedback.
Automating the entire machine learning pipeline, including model building and training, is needed to democratize data science and reduce the reliance on specialized expertise.
results: DS-Agent achieved a 100% success rate with GPT-4 in the development stage, reliably building bug-free ML models, and significantly outperformed other LLM agents like ResearchAgent.

In the deployment stage, DS-Agent demonstrated near 100% one-pass code generation with GPT-4 and an 85% one-pass rate with GPT-3.5, while reducing operational costs by over 90% compared to the development stage.
The system consistently improved performance over iterations in the development stage and showed competitive performance with or surpassed traditional AutoML systems like AutoGluon in tabular tasks.
solution: DS-Agent introduces a novel two-stage framework combining LLMs with Case-Based Reasoning (CBR), leveraging a 'human insight case bank' from Kaggle and an 'agent case bank' of successful past solutions.

The development stage employs an iterative CBR loop (Retrieve, ReviseRank, Reuse, Execute, Retain) with an LLM-powered `ReviseRanker` that dynamically adjusts the ranking of retrieved cases based on execution feedback.

The deployment stage uses a simplified CBR approach, retrieving the most similar successful agent-generated code from the 'agent case bank' and using an `Adapter` LLM to directly generate code for new tasks, enabling low-resource efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: DS-Agent is an automated data science framework that empowers large language models (LLMs) with Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) to build and train machine learning models. The system achieved a 100% success rate in model construction during its development phase and reduced deployment costs by over 90% while maintaining high reliability in code generation.

title:
DS-Agent: Automated Data Science by Empowering Large Language Models
  with Case-Based Reasoning

abstract: Automating AI research differs from general software engineering due to computationally expensive evaluation (e.g., model training) and opaque performance attribution. Current LLM-based agents struggle here, often generating monolithic scripts that ignore execution costs and causal factors. We introduce MARS (Modular Agent with Reflective Search), a framework optimized for autonomous AI research. MARS relies on three pillars: (1) Budget-Aware Planning via cost-constrained Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to explicitly balance performance with execution expense; (2) Modular Construction, employing a "Design-Decompose-Implement" pipeline to manage complex research repositories; and (3) Comparative Reflective Memory, which addresses credit assignment by analyzing solution differences to distill high-signal insights. MARS achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source frameworks on MLE-Bench under comparable settings, maintaining competitiveness with the global leaderboard's top methods. Furthermore, the system exhibits qualitative "Aha!" moments, where 63% of all utilized lessons originate from cross-branch transfer, demonstrating that the agent effectively generalizes insights across search paths.
title: MARS: Modular Agent with Reflective Search for Automated AI Research

abstract:
We present a novel reasoning approach called Flow-of-Options (FoO), designed
to address intrinsic biases in Large Language Models (LLMs). Flow-of-Options
enables LLMs to systematically explore a diverse range of possibilities in
their reasoning, as demonstrated by an FoO-based agentic framework developed
for autonomously solving Machine Learning (ML) tasks. FoO enforces diversity in
LLM solutions through compressed and interpretable task representations,
resulting in improvements of 38.2% - 69.2% on standard data science tasks, and
37.4% - 47.9% on therapeutic chemistry tasks, as compared to state-of-the-art
baselines. With an overall operation cost under $1 per task, our framework is
well-suited for cost-sensitive applications. Going beyond tabular
classification and regression, we show the broader applicability of our
FoO-based agentic system to tasks such as reinforcement learning and image
generation. Our code is open-sourced at:
this https URL
title: Flow-of-Options: Diversified and Improved LLM Reasoning by Thinking  Through Options

abstract: Deep research is emerging as a representative long-horizon task for large language model (LLM) agents. However, long trajectories in deep research often exceed model context limits, compressing token budgets for both evidence collection and report writing, and preventing effective test-time scaling. We introduce FS-Researcher, a file-system-based, dual-agent framework that scales deep research beyond the context window via a persistent workspace. Specifically, a Context Builder agent acts as a librarian which browses the internet, writes structured notes, and archives raw sources into a hierarchical knowledge base that can grow far beyond context length. A Report Writer agent then composes the final report section by section, treating the knowledge base as the source of facts. In this framework, the file system serves as a durable external memory and a shared coordination medium across agents and sessions, enabling iterative refinement beyond the context window. Experiments on two open-ended benchmarks (DeepResearch Bench and DeepConsult) show that FS-Researcher achieves state-of-the-art report quality across different backbone models. Further analyses demonstrate a positive correlation between final report quality and the computation allocated to the Context Builder, validating effective test-time scaling under the file-system paradigm. The code and data are anonymously open-sourced at this https URL.

keyInsights: Employing a persistent, structured file system workspace effectively circumvents LLM context window constraints, allowing for virtually unlimited context size and comprehensive evidence accumulation in long-horizon tasks.

Decoupling the evidence gathering and report writing processes into specialized agents significantly enhances research quality by preventing premature synthesis and enabling deeper, more focused exploration.

The framework demonstrates test-time scaling, empirically showing that allocating more computational rounds to the Context Builder directly correlates with a richer knowledge base and improved overall report quality.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) face inherent context window limitations, hindering their ability to process vast information and long reasoning chains required for complex, long-horizon deep research tasks.
Existing context compression techniques often lead to 'lossy bottlenecks' by discarding fine-grained evidence, causing error accumulation and incomplete information coverage in research reports.
Traditional LLM agent designs treat internal states and tool observations as ephemeral, preventing iterative refinement and the accumulation of knowledge over extended task durations.
results: FS-Researcher, using Claude-Sonnet-4.5, achieved a RACE score of 53.94 on the DeepResearch Bench, surpassing the strongest baseline (RhinoInsight) by +3.02, with notable gains in Comprehensiveness (+3.74) and Insight (+4.4).

Increasing Context Builder rounds from 3 to 10 led to a larger and richer knowledge base (more archived sources, notes, citations) and consistently improved report quality (Comprehensiveness, Insight, Instruction Following) on the DeepResearch Bench.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical impact of architectural components: removing the persistent workspace resulted in a -4.07 RACE degradation, merging agents caused a -10.35 RACE drop, and one-shot report writing led to a -5.13 RACE decline.
solution: A dual-agent, file-system-based framework is proposed, utilizing a shared, persistent workspace as an external memory and coordination medium for managing research state and evidence.

The framework employs two specialized agents: a Context Builder for internet browsing, information distillation, and structured knowledge base construction, and a Report Writer for composing the final report solely from the built knowledge base.

Agents interact with the workspace using file system tools and web browsing tools, guided by control files (Todos, Checklist, Logs) to enable multi-session iterative refinement of the knowledge base and report drafts.
paper_summary.summary: FS-Researcher introduces a dual-agent, file-system-based framework that addresses large language model context window limitations for deep research by enabling test-time scaling through a persistent workspace. This approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming previous baselines on the DeepResearch Bench and DeepConsult by improving report comprehensiveness and insight.

title: FS-Researcher: Test-Time Scaling for Long-Horizon Research Tasks with File-System-Based Agents

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are catalyzing the development of autonomous AI research agents for scientific and engineering discovery. We present FM Agent, a novel and general-purpose multi-agent framework that leverages a synergistic combination of LLM-based reasoning and large-scale evolutionary search to address complex real-world challenges. The core of FM Agent integrates several key innovations: 1) a cold-start initialization phase incorporating expert guidance, 2) a novel evolutionary sampling strategy for iterative optimization, 3) domain-specific evaluators that combine correctness, effectiveness, and LLM-supervised feedback, and 4) a distributed, asynchronous execution infrastructure built on Ray. Demonstrating broad applicability, our system has been evaluated across diverse domains, including operations research, machine learning, GPU kernel optimization, and classical mathematical problems. FM Agent reaches state-of-the-art results autonomously, without human interpretation or tuning -- 1976.3 on ALE-Bench (+5.2\%), 43.56\% on MLE-Bench (+4.0pp), up to 20x speedups on KernelBench, and establishes new state-of-the-art(SOTA) results on several classical mathematical problems. Beyond academic benchmarks, FM Agent shows considerable promise for both large-scale enterprise R\&D workflows and fundamental scientific research, where it can accelerate innovation, automate complex discovery processes, and deliver substantial engineering and scientific advances with broader societal impact.
title: The FM Agent

abstract: The automation of scientific discovery represents a critical milestone in Artificial Intelligence (AI) research. However, existing agentic systems for science suffer from two fundamental limitations: rigid, pre-programmed workflows that cannot adapt to intermediate findings, and inadequate context management that hinders long-horizon research. We present \texttt{freephdlabor}, an open-source multiagent framework featuring \textit{fully dynamic workflows} determined by real-time agent reasoning and a \coloremph{\textit{modular architecture}} enabling seamless customization -- users can modify, add, or remove agents to address domain-specific requirements. The framework provides comprehensive infrastructure including \textit{automatic context compaction}, \textit{workspace-based communication} to prevent information degradation, \textit{memory persistence} across sessions, and \textit{non-blocking human intervention} mechanisms. These features collectively transform automated research from isolated, single-run attempts into \textit{continual research programs} that build systematically on prior explorations and incorporate human feedback. By providing both the architectural principles and practical implementation for building customizable co-scientist systems, this work aims to facilitate broader adoption of automated research across scientific domains, enabling practitioners to deploy interactive multiagent systems that autonomously conduct end-to-end research -- from ideation through experimentation to publication-ready manuscripts.

keyInsights: Dynamic workflows emerging from real-time agent reasoning enable true adaptability and responsiveness in scientific discovery, unlike rigid pre-programmed pipelines.
Robust, lossless communication via a shared file-based workspace is essential to prevent information degradation in complex, long-horizon multi-agent interactions.
A modular, open-source design significantly lowers barriers to entry, allowing scientists to customize AI agents for specific domain needs and fostering wider adoption.
originalProblem: Existing AI science automation systems rely on rigid, pre-programmed workflows, hindering adaptability to evolving research contexts or unexpected findings.
Large Language Models suffer from limited context windows, leading to information loss and distortion over iterative interactions in long-horizon scientific tasks.
Inter-agent communication in multi-agent systems often results in 'information degradation' or the 'game of telephone effect,' impeding reliable coordination.
results: Successfully completed an end-to-end research project on 'HMM-based Training Phase Detection,' from ideation to a publication-ready manuscript.
Demonstrated dynamic error recovery, where the ManagerAgent autonomously identified and resolved a workspace configuration issue without human intervention.

Achieved quality-driven iteration, utilizing a ReviewerAgent to assess paper drafts and orchestrating comprehensive revisions, ultimately improving the paper's quality to an 'Accept with Minor Revisions' equivalent.
solution: Employs a star-shaped multi-agent architecture with a central ManagerAgent that dynamically orchestrates tasks using the ReAct framework, adapting workflows in real-time.
Utilizes a shared, file-based workspace for inter-agent communication, ensuring lossless data exchange and acting as persistent external memory.
Incorporates features for continual research, including automatic context compaction, memory persistence, and non-blocking human intervention for long-term collaboration.
paper_summary.summary: freephdlabor introduces an open-source, multi-agent framework that orchestrates dynamic scientific research workflows, enabling AI agents to conduct continuous, interactive, and customizable end-to-end discovery. This system demonstrates its capability by autonomously completing a research project, including error recovery and quality-driven revisions, from ideation to a publication-ready manuscript.

title: Build Your Personalized Research Group: A Multiagent Framework for Continual and Interactive Science Automation

abstract: AI agents increasingly operate over extended time horizons, yet their ability to retain, organize, and recall multimodal experiences remains a critical bottleneck. Building effective lifelong memory requires navigating a vast design space spanning architecture, retrieval strategies, prompt engineering, and data pipelines; this space is too large and interconnected for manual exploration or traditional AutoML to explore effectively. We deploy an autonomous research pipeline to discover Omni-SimpleMem, a unified multimodal memory framework for lifelong AI agents. Starting from a naïve baseline (F1=0.117 on LoCoMo), the pipeline autonomously executes ${\sim}50$ experiments across two benchmarks, diagnosing failure modes, proposing architectural modifications, and repairing data pipeline bugs, all without human intervention in the inner loop. The resulting system achieves state-of-the-art on both benchmarks, improving F1 by +411% on LoCoMo (0.117$\to$0.598) and +214% on Mem-Gallery (0.254$\to$0.797) relative to the initial configurations. Critically, the most impactful discoveries are not hyperparameter adjustments: bug fixes (+175%), architectural changes (+44%), and prompt engineering (+188% on specific categories) each individually exceed the cumulative contribution of all hyperparameter tuning, demonstrating capabilities fundamentally beyond the reach of traditional AutoML. We provide a taxonomy of six discovery types and identify four properties that make multimodal memory particularly suited for autoresearch, offering guidance for applying autonomous research pipelines to other AI system domains. Code is available at this this https URL.

keyInsights: Autonomous research pipelines can effectively discover and optimize complex AI systems by performing architectural redesigns, diagnosing/fixing bugs (e.g., +175% F1 from one-line fix), and refining prompt engineering, extending beyond traditional hyperparameter tuning.
The most impactful performance gains in complex systems often stem from structural changes, bug fixes, and prompt refinements rather than cumulative hyperparameter adjustments.

Multimodal memory is particularly suitable for autonomous research due to immediate scalar evaluation metrics, a modular architecture, fast iteration cycles, and version-controlled code modifications.
originalProblem: AI agents struggle to effectively retain, organize, and recall the diverse, multimodal experiences accumulated during lifelong interactions.
Existing memory systems are often unimodal or fragmented, neglect rich visual and auditory signals, and face scalability or retrieval quality degradation.
Manual research for complex AI systems is slow and limited, while traditional AutoML cannot perform architectural redesigns or complex bug fixes crucial for optimization.
results: The autonomous pipeline successfully improved LoCoMo F1 from 0.117 to 0.598 (+411%) and Mem-Gallery F1 from 0.254 to 0.797 (+214%) over approximately 72 hours, demonstrating high-impact bug fixes and architectural discoveries.

OMNI-SIMPLEMEM achieved state-of-the-art F1 scores of up to 0.613 on LoCoMo and 0.810 on Mem-Gallery, consistently outperforming leading baselines by substantial margins (e.g., >25 percentage points on Mem-Gallery).
The system demonstrated 3.5 times faster throughput than the fastest baseline (5.81 queries/second with 8 workers) due to its architecture supporting concurrent lookups using read-only indices.
solution: An autonomous research pipeline, AUTORESEARCHCLAW, was deployed to iteratively discover and optimize OMNI-SIMPLEMEM, starting from a text-only memory framework.

The pipeline analyzes performance deficiencies, generates hypotheses, implements code changes (architectural, retrieval, prompt, data pipeline), executes experiments, and evaluates using quantitative metrics.

OMNI-SIMPLEMEM features selective novelty-based multimodal ingestion into Multimodal Atomic Units (MAUs), two-tier hot/cold storage, progressive pyramid retrieval with hybrid dense-sparse search, and knowledge graph-augmented retrieval.
paper_summary.summary: An autonomous research pipeline generated OMNI-SIMPLEMEM, a unified multimodal memory framework that enables lifelong AI agents to effectively manage diverse experiences across text, images, audio, and video. This system achieved state-of-the-art F1 scores up to 0.613 on LoCoMo and 0.810 on Mem-Gallery benchmarks, significantly outperforming previous baselines, while also demonstrating 3.5x faster throughput.

title: Omni-SimpleMem: Autoresearch-Guided Discovery of Lifelong Multimodal Agent Memory

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have shown significant autonomous capabilities in dynamically searching and incorporating relevant tools or Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers for individual queries. However, fixed context windows limit effectiveness in multi-turn interactions requiring repeated, independent tool usage. We introduce MemTool, a short-term memory framework enabling LLM agents to dynamically manage tools or MCP server contexts across multi-turn conversations. MemTool offers three agentic architectures: 1) Autonomous Agent Mode, granting full tool management autonomy, 2) Workflow Mode, providing deterministic control without autonomy, and 3) Hybrid Mode, combining autonomous and deterministic control. Evaluating each MemTool mode across 13+ LLMs on the ScaleMCP benchmark, we conducted experiments over 100 consecutive user interactions, measuring tool removal ratios (short-term memory efficiency) and task completion accuracy. In Autonomous Agent Mode, reasoning LLMs achieve high tool-removal efficiency (90-94% over a 3-window average), while medium-sized models exhibit significantly lower efficiency (0-60%). Workflow and Hybrid modes consistently manage tool removal effectively, whereas Autonomous and Hybrid modes excel at task completion. We present trade-offs and recommendations for each MemTool mode based on task accuracy, agency, and model capabilities.

keyInsights: Granting LLM agents full autonomy for tool management is effective only for highly capable, "reasoning" models, while less capable models struggle with tool removal and lead to context bloat.
Deterministic workflow-based approaches (Workflow and Hybrid modes) consistently achieve high tool removal efficiency (over 90%) across various LLMs, reliably preventing context window overflow.

Explicitly including the current tool count in the system prompt is crucial for LLMs to effectively manage and remove tools in autonomous settings, suggesting this information is not inherently inferred.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) have finite context windows, which become a bottleneck when dynamically managing a growing list of tools over multi-turn conversations.
Current LLM memory management strategies primarily address conversational history (e.g., summarization), neglecting the dynamic addition and removal of tools from the agent's context.
LLM agents face context window overflow and degraded performance as tools accumulate over prolonged interactions, hindering their practical deployment in dynamic environments.
results: High-performing LLMs like GPT-o3 and Gemini 2.5 Pro in Autonomous Agent Mode achieved 90-94% average tool removal efficiency and 80-90% task completion.
Both Workflow and Hybrid modes consistently demonstrated over 90% average tool removal efficiency across nearly all evaluated LLMs, effectively preventing tool accumulation below the 128-tool limit.
Hybrid Mode generally yielded superior task completion, with models such as Claude 3.7 Sonnet reaching 88%, by balancing deterministic pruning with agentic tool discovery.
solution: MemTool proposes a short-term memory framework with three architectural modes: Autonomous Agent, Workflow, and Hybrid, to dynamically manage an LLM agent's tool context.
The Autonomous Agent mode empowers LLMs with special `Search_Tools` and `Remove_Tools` functions for self-directed tool management within their context window.

Workflow mode utilizes separate, sequential LLM calls for deterministic tool pruning and search, abstracting management from the main agent's turn, while Hybrid mode combines deterministic pruning with agentic tool addition.
paper_summary.summary: MemTool introduces a short-term memory framework for Large Language Model (LLM) agents, enabling dynamic management of tool context across multi-turn conversations through three architectural modes. An evaluation across 13 diverse LLMs and 100 multi-turn interactions demonstrates how these modes achieve high tool removal efficiency, with specific models in Autonomous and Hybrid modes exhibiting over 90% average tool removal ratio, preventing context window overflow and maintaining task performance.

title: MemTool: Optimizing Short-Term Memory Management for Dynamic Tool Calling in LLM Agent Multi-Turn Conversations

abstract: Deep research has emerged as a transformative capability for autonomous agents, empowering Large Language Models to navigate complex, open-ended tasks. However, realizing its full potential is hindered by critical limitations, including escalating contextual noise in long-horizon tasks, fragility leading to cascading errors, and a lack of modular extensibility. To address these challenges, we introduce Yunque DeepResearch, a hierarchical, modular, and robust framework. The architecture is characterized by three key components: (1) a centralized Multi-Agent Orchestration System that routes subtasks to an Atomic Capability Pool of tools and specialized sub-agents; (2) a Dynamic Context Management mechanism that structures completed sub-goals into semantic summaries to mitigate information overload; and (3) a proactive Supervisor Module that ensures resilience through active anomaly detection and context pruning. Yunque DeepResearch achieves state-of-the-art performance across a range of agentic deep research benchmarks, including GAIA, BrowseComp, BrowseComp-ZH, and Humanity's Last Exam. We open-source the framework, reproducible implementations, and application cases to empower the community.

keyInsights: Decoupling atomic capabilities from central reasoning through a hierarchical architecture improves robustness and generality for autonomous agents.

Dynamic context management, which synthesizes completed sub-goals into concise summaries and retains fine-grained traces for active ones, effectively mitigates cognitive overload in long-horizon tasks.
A dedicated Supervisor module performing active anomaly detection and self-correction is critical for preventing cascading failures and ensuring stable task execution.
originalProblem: Large language models traditionally lack the agency required for active information discovery, verification, and synthesis in dynamic environments.
Existing monolithic agent architectures face cognitive overload in long-horizon tasks, leading to context window saturation and degraded reasoning performance.
Current autonomous agents are often fragile, prone to cascading failures and lacking modularity for integrating specialized tools.
results: The framework achieved state-of-the-art performance on BrowseComp (62.5%), BrowseComp-ZH (75.9%), and Humanity’s Last Exam (51.7%), and a competitive second place on GAIA (78.6%).

It improved baseline LLM performance, boosting Gemini 3 Pro by 10.0% on BrowseComp and 4.8% on GAIA compared to a standard ReAct approach, demonstrating generalizability across different backbone models.

Ablation studies confirmed that dynamic context management, the Supervisor module, and specialized agents are crucial, with memory removal causing the largest performance drop (e.g., -10.4% on BrowseComp).
solution: A hierarchical, modular framework that explicitly decouples atomic capabilities from central reasoning, featuring a Main Agent for orchestration and dynamic planning.
A Context Manager implementing dual-level memory with structured synthesis and dynamic context management to combat cognitive overload.
An Atomic Capability Pool providing specialized sub-agents (e.g., GUI, data analysis) and basic tools, alongside a Supervisor for adaptive self-correction and anomaly detection.
paper_summary.summary: The Yunque DeepResearch framework, developed by Tencent BAC and academic partners, empowers large language models with autonomous deep research capabilities through a hierarchical, modular, and robust design. This system secured state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks, including BrowseComp and Humanity’s Last Exam, and improved underlying LLM performance by up to 10.0% on BrowseComp.

title: Yunque DeepResearch Technical Report

abstract: Autonomous agents powered by large language models (LLMs) show significant potential for achieving high autonomy in various scenarios such as software development. Recent research has shown that LLM agents can leverage past experiences to reduce errors and enhance efficiency. However, the static experience paradigm, reliant on a fixed collection of past experiences acquired heuristically, lacks iterative refinement and thus hampers agents' adaptability. In this paper, we introduce the Iterative Experience Refinement framework, enabling LLM agents to refine experiences iteratively during task execution. We propose two fundamental patterns: the successive pattern, refining based on nearest experiences within a task batch, and the cumulative pattern, acquiring experiences across all previous task batches. Augmented with our heuristic experience elimination, the method prioritizes high-quality and frequently-used experiences, effectively managing the experience space and enhancing efficiency. Extensive experiments show that while the successive pattern may yield superior results, the cumulative pattern provides more stable performance. Moreover, experience elimination facilitates achieving better performance using just 11.54% of a high-quality subset.

author_info.institution: King's College London, University of London

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Experience propagation can follow successive (inheriting from the latest predecessor) or cumulative (integrating all historical experiences) patterns, each offering distinct performance characteristics (peak vs. stable performance).

A heuristic experience elimination mechanism, based on information gain and utilization frequency, can significantly reduce the experience pool size (to 11.54%) while maintaining or improving quality metrics.
Experiences acquired from a predecessor task batch remain relevant for descendant batches, though their utilization frequency tends to decrease for more distant descendants.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based software development agents primarily rely on static experience paradigms, where their knowledge base is fixed.
The static nature of experience limits the adaptability and effectiveness of these agents when confronted with new and evolving software development challenges.
Without continuous learning, agents cannot improve their performance over time or effectively manage growing experience pools.
results: The Iterative Experience Refinement (IER) framework improved software quality by up to 11% compared to existing baselines like ECL, GPT-Engineer, MetaGPT, and ChatDev when tested on the SRDD dataset.
Efficiency metrics (review and test) consistently improved over subsequent task batches, demonstrating the effectiveness of iterative refinement for long-term agent improvement.

The experience elimination mechanism successfully maintained or surpassed quality metrics while reducing the experience pool size to 11.54% of its original, demonstrating improved resource efficiency.
solution: The Iterative Experience Refinement (IER) framework enables LLM-based software development agents to continually refine their experiences, shifting from static to dynamic experience paradigms.
The framework employs multi-agent collaboration (instructive and responsive agents) to generate software solutions, along with task batching to manage experience propagation.
It acquires 'shortcuts' (efficient experiences) from agent communication chains using a self-instruction mechanism and utilizes these experiences via retrieval during task execution.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University, Dalian University of Technology, Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications, and Siemens propose an Iterative Experience Refinement (IER) framework, allowing LLM-based software development agents to continually learn and improve by dynamically refining their experience pools. This approach leads to up to 11% relative improvement in software quality and consistent efficiency gains over baselines, while an experience elimination mechanism efficiently manages the growing experience space.

title: Iterative Experience Refinement of Software-Developing Agents

abstract:
We present AutoResearch-RL, a framework in which a reinforcement learning agent conducts open-ended neural architecture and hyperparameter research without human supervision, running perpetually until a termination oracle signals convergence or resource exhaustion. At each step the agent proposes a code modification to a target training script, executes it under a fixed wall clock time budget, observes a scalar reward derived from validation bits-per-byte (val-bpb), and updates its policy via Proximal Policy Optimisation (PPO).
